subject: math
title: PSLE Math — Speed, Distance & Time (multi-stage problems)

stats.headline: Speed problems are worth 3-5 marks each on PSLE Paper 2. Most are multi-stage — one object with two speeds, two objects catching up or meeting, or a return journey. Same 4 patterns repeat every year. Spot the pattern → write the right equation in one line.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Speed, Distance & Time
keyConcepts.body: Speed × Time = Distance. That's the whole formula. The trap isn't the formula — it's that PSLE almost always chains TWO stages or TWO objects, and students apply the formula to the wrong combination of numbers.
bullets:
**The formula triangle :**
-- Distance = **Speed × Time**
-- Speed = **Distance ÷ Time**
-- Time = **Distance ÷ Speed**
💡

**The 4 patterns :**
-- 🔵 **Pattern A** — multi-stage -- 🟢 **Pattern B** — same direction, catching up -- 🟠 **Pattern C** — opposite direction, meeting -- 🟣 **Pattern D** — return journey
**3-question check**

**The formula triangle to memorise:**
-- Distance = **Speed × Time**
-- Speed = **Distance ÷ Time**
-- Time = **Distance ÷ Speed**
💡 Cover the unknown — the other two tell you whether to multiply or divide.

**The 4 patterns PSLE rotates:**
-- 🔵 **Pattern A** — one object, multi-stage (walked then ran).
-- 🟢 **Pattern B** — two objects, same direction, catching up.
-- 🟠 **Pattern C** — two objects, opposite direction, meeting.
-- 🟣 **Pattern D** — one object, return journey — the average-speed trap.
Next slide is the **3-question check** to figure out which pattern the problem is throwing at you — and how to handle units before you start.

keyConcepts.title: The 3-question check — every speed problem
keyConcepts.body: Run these BEFORE drawing any model or writing any formula. They take 10 seconds and tell you which pattern you're facing and what units to lock in.
bullets:
**Q1 — One object or TWO?**
-- one object **Pattern A** (multi-stage) -- two objects →

**Q2 — Same direction or opposite?**
-- **Pattern B** subtract speeds -- **Pattern C** add speeds -- one object goes and returns **Pattern D**

**Q3 — Are the units consistent?**
-- km/h with hours → ✓ get km.
-- km/h with minutes → ✗ -- m/s with km → ✗
💡 ** ** **S**ame direction → **S**ubtract. **O**pposite → add.

Question **Q1 — One object or TWO?**
-- If one object with a speed change → **Pattern A** (multi-stage).
-- If two objects → go to Q2.

Question **Q2 — Same direction or opposite?**
-- Same direction → **Pattern B** — subtract speeds (closing speed = faster − slower).
-- Opposite direction → **Pattern C** — add speeds (closing speed = sum).
-- Special case — one object goes and returns → **Pattern D**.

Question **Q3 — Are the units consistent?**
-- km/h with hours → ✓ get km.
-- km/h with minutes → ✗ convert to hours OR convert km/h to km/min first.
-- m/s with km → ✗ convert before solving — most-missed step in this whole topic.
💡 **Memory trick:** **S**ame direction → **S**ubtract. **O**pposite → add. (The S–S match keeps the rule sticky.)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — One object, multi-stage
keyConcepts.body: Clue words: "then", "for the rest of", "remaining distance". One person/car/train but the speed changes partway. Solve each stage separately, then combine.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example :**
Eric took **2 hours** to travel **3/5** of his journey.
He completed the remaining **84 km** in **1 hour**. **average speed for the whole journey**

** **
Remaining 2/5 = 84 km → 1/5 = 42 km → **whole = 210 km**

** **
Stage 1 = 2 h, Stage 2 = 1 h → **total time = 3 h**
⚠️ ** ** Stage 1 speed Stage 2 speed total distance ÷ total time

**📝 Worked example from PSLE 2018:**
Eric took **2 hours** to travel **3/5** of his journey.
He completed the remaining **84 km** in **1 hour**.
What was Eric's **average speed for the whole journey**?

**Step 1 — Find the whole journey.**
Remaining 2/5 = 84 km → 1/5 = 42 km → **whole = 210 km**.

**Step 2 — Find total time.**
Stage 1 = 2 h, Stage 2 = 1 h → **total time = 3 h**.

**Step 3 — Average speed = total distance ÷ total time.**
210 ÷ 3 = **70 km/h**.
⚠️ **Trap to avoid:** students try to average Stage 1 speed and Stage 2 speed directly. That's wrong (see Pattern D for why). Always use total distance ÷ total time for average speed.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Same direction (catching up)
keyConcepts.body: Clue words: "Jane was ahead of Linda", "started X minutes earlier". Two objects moving in the SAME direction. The faster one is closing a gap at a speed = (faster − slower).
bullets:
**📝 Worked example :**
When Jane started jogging from her home, Linda was already
**1.2 km ahead** of her.
Jane jogged at **10 km/h** and Linda walked at **4 km/h**

** **
Same direction → SUBTRACT: 10 − 4 = **6 km/h**

** **
Time to close 1.2 km gap = 1.2 ÷ 6 = **0.2 h** = **12 minutes**
⚠️ ** ** closing a 1.2 km gap closing speed

**📝 Worked example from PSLE 2022:**
When Jane started jogging from her home, Linda was already
**1.2 km ahead** of her.
Jane jogged at **10 km/h** and Linda walked at **4 km/h**.
How long did Jane take to catch up with Linda?

**Step 1 — Find the closing speed.**
Same direction → SUBTRACT: 10 − 4 = **6 km/h** (this is how fast Jane gains on Linda).

**Step 2 — Use Time = Distance ÷ Speed.**
Time to close 1.2 km gap = 1.2 ÷ 6 = **0.2 h** = **12 minutes**.

⚠️ **Trap to avoid:** students use Jane's speed alone (10 km/h) and write 1.2 ÷ 10 = 0.12 h. But Jane isn't crossing 1.2 km — she's closing a 1.2 km gap while Linda also moves. Use the closing speed, not either person's actual speed.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Opposite direction (meeting)
keyConcepts.body: Clue words: "in opposite directions", "from each end", "from opposite ends of a track". Two objects moving toward each other. They close the gap at a speed = (object 1 + object 2).
bullets:
**📝 Worked example:**
Two trains start from cities **360 km apart** and travel
toward each other along the same track.
Train A travels at **80 km/h** and Train B travels at **100 km/h**

** **
Opposite direction → ADD: 80 + 100 = **180 km/h**
⚠️ ** ** same direction

**📝 Worked example:**
Two trains start from cities **360 km apart** and travel
toward each other along the same track.
Train A travels at **80 km/h** and Train B travels at **100 km/h**.
How long until they meet?

**Step 1 — Find the closing speed.**
Opposite direction → ADD: 80 + 100 = **180 km/h** (every hour, the gap shrinks by 180 km).

**Step 2 — Use Time = Distance ÷ Speed.**
Time = 360 ÷ 180 = **2 hours**.
💡 **Why add?** Both trains are eating into the gap from their own ends. Train A closes 80 km per hour, Train B closes another 100 km per hour — together they wipe out 180 km per hour.
⚠️ **Trap to avoid:** students subtract speeds (100 − 80 = 20) and write 360 ÷ 20 = 18 h. That would only be correct if they were going the same direction (Pattern B). Opposite direction always ADDS.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern A — Multi-stage
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Mr Lim drove 90 km at 60 km/h, then drove another 60 km at 40 km/h.
What was his average speed for the whole journey?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Average speed = total distance ÷ total time. Total distance = 90 + 60 = 150 km. Stage 1 time = 90 ÷ 60 = 1.5 h. Stage 2 time = 60 ÷ 40 = 1.5 h. Total time = 3 h. Average = 150 ÷ 3 = 50 km/h. (Naive "average of 60 and 40 = 50" only works by coincidence here because the two stages took equal time — usually it wouldn't!)

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern B — Catching up
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Ahmad cycled from his house at 18 km/h. Ben started cycling from
the same house 20 minutes later, at 24 km/h, along the same road.
How long after Ben started will he catch up with Ahmad?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: By the time Ben starts, Ahmad is already 18 × (20/60) = 6 km ahead. Same direction → closing speed = 24 − 18 = 6 km/h. Time to close 6 km = 6 ÷ 6 = 1 h = 60 minutes.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Return journey (average-speed trap)
keyConcepts.body: Clue words: "drove to town X at A km/h and returned at B km/h", "round trip". Same distance each way, but DIFFERENT speeds. The headline trap is averaging the two speeds directly — that gives the WRONG answer.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example:**
Mr Tan drove from town A to town B at **60 km/h** and
returned along the same road at **40 km/h**

** **
Going: 120 ÷ 60 = 2 h. Returning: 120 ÷ 40 = 3 h.
Total time = **5 h**

** **
Total distance = 120 × 2 = 240 km.
Average = 240 ÷ 5 = **48 km/h**

**📝 Worked example:**
Mr Tan drove from town A to town B at **60 km/h** and
returned along the same road at **40 km/h**.
Find his average speed for the whole journey.
**⚠️ The trap answer:** (60 + 40) ÷ 2 = 50 km/h. Wrong — and almost every student writes this.

**Step 1 — Assume any distance for one way.**
Pick a number that divides nicely. Let one-way distance = **120 km**.

**Step 2 — Compute times for each leg.**
Going: 120 ÷ 60 = 2 h. Returning: 120 ÷ 40 = 3 h.
Total time = **5 h**.

**Step 3 — Average speed = total distance ÷ total time.**
Total distance = 120 × 2 = 240 km.
Average = 240 ÷ 5 = **48 km/h** (not 50).

💡 **Why is it less than 50?** You spent MORE time at the slower speed (3 h at 40 km/h vs 2 h at 60 km/h), so the slower speed gets more "weight" in the average. The trick of assuming a distance works for ANY value — try 240 km, you'd still get 48 km/h.

keyConcepts.title: Top 5 traps to avoid
keyConcepts.body: The most-missed mistakes across all speed problems. Read these once before starting practice — they save 2-3 marks per paper.
bullets: ⚠️ ** 1 — Averaging two speeds directly for average speed.** total distance ÷ total time

⚠️ ** 2 — Mixing up add vs subtract for two objects.**
Same direction → subtract speeds. Opposite direction → add speeds. **S**ame → **S**ubtract.
⚠️ ** 3 — Mixing units mid-problem.** (45 min = 0.75 h) (60 km/h = 1 km/min)
⚠️ ** 4 — Forgetting the head start in staggered-start problems.**
⚠️ ** 5 — Using one object's speed instead of the closing speed.** closing speed

⚠️ **Trap 1 — Averaging two speeds directly for average speed.**
Average speed = total distance ÷ total time, NOT (speed₁ + speed₂) ÷ 2. This is the most common Paper 2 mistake.

⚠️ **Trap 2 — Mixing up add vs subtract for two objects.**
Same direction → subtract speeds. Opposite direction → add speeds.
Mantra: **S**ame → **S**ubtract.

⚠️ **Trap 3 — Mixing units mid-problem.**
km/h with minutes won't work. Either convert minutes to hours (45 min = 0.75 h), or convert km/h to km/min (60 km/h = 1 km/min). Lock units BEFORE doing any arithmetic.

⚠️ **Trap 4 — Forgetting the head start in staggered-start problems.**
"Robot B started 7 minutes AFTER Robot A" — Robot A had already moved for 7 minutes. Compute Robot A's lead distance first, THEN apply catching-up logic.

⚠️ **Trap 5 — Using one object's speed instead of the closing speed.**
When two objects move together, you almost never use just one of their speeds. Use the closing speed — sum or difference — to figure out the time to meet/catch up.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern C — Meeting (opposite direction)
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Jack and Kira start at the same point on a 6 km circular track
at 8.00 a.m. and walk in opposite directions. Jack walks at
5 km/h and Kira walks at 4 km/h. At what time will they first
meet?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Opposite direction → ADD speeds. Closing speed = 5 + 4 = 9 km/h. They meet when their combined distance = full track = 6 km. Time = 6 ÷ 9 = 2/3 h = 40 min. Meeting time = 8.00 + 40 min = 8.40 a.m. (Trap distractor: 10.40 a.m. comes from using the DIFFERENCE 1 km/h → 6 ÷ 1 = 6 h. That's the same-direction rule wrongly applied here.)

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern D — Return journey
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Mei drove 120 km to Malacca at 80 km/h and returned along the
same road at 60 km/h. What was her average speed for the
whole trip?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Going: 120 ÷ 80 = 1.5 h. Returning: 120 ÷ 60 = 2 h. Total time = 3.5 h. Total distance = 240 km. Average speed = 240 ÷ 3.5 = 68.57… ≈ 68.6 km/h. (Distractor 70 = (80 + 60) ÷ 2 — the classic naive-average trap.)

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from real PSLE-style speed problems. Trains the reflex of running the 3-question check FIRST, locking units SECOND, and only THEN writing the formula.
bullets: **mix of 10 questions** multi-stage, catching up, meeting, return journey
Wrong answers auto-saved reviewed in 1 week
AI marks partial credit
You'll tackle a **mix of 10 questions** across all four patterns — multi-stage, catching up, meeting, and return journey.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your working step-by-step — partial credit if you set up the right closing speed but slipped on arithmetic.

keyWords.definition: Speed × Time = Distance. To find Speed: cover S in the triangle → Distance ÷ Time. To find Time: cover T → Distance ÷ Speed. The visual helps you not mix up which to multiply vs divide.
How fast the gap between two moving objects shrinks. Same direction → faster − slower. Opposite direction → sum of both speeds. Use this to find time-to-meet, never one object's actual speed alone.
Total distance ÷ total time. Almost NEVER equal to (speed₁ + speed₂) ÷ 2. The slower stage takes more time so it weighs more — that's why a 60/40 return trip averages 48, not 50.
When one object starts moving before the other. Compute the head-start distance (first object's speed × delay time) FIRST, then apply catching-up logic with the closing speed.
Same direction → Subtract. Memory hook for which arithmetic to use on the two objects' speeds. Opposite direction → Add (S+S also works: Sum for Opposite Sides).

subTopics.label: Pattern A — One object, multi-stage
subTopics.description: One person/vehicle with two or more speeds across the journey. Solve each stage separately, then average = total distance ÷ total time.

subTopics.label: Pattern B — Same direction (catching up)
subTopics.description: Two objects moving the same way. Closing speed = faster − slower. Time to catch up = gap ÷ closing speed.

subTopics.label: Pattern C — Opposite direction (meeting)
subTopics.description: Two objects moving toward each other or away from a shared point. Closing speed = sum of speeds. Combined distance covered = full gap when they meet.

subTopics.label: Pattern D — Return journey (average speed)
subTopics.description: Same distance, different speeds each way. Average speed = total distance ÷ total time. Never (speed₁ + speed₂) ÷ 2.
